notes: Tenth validated clue batch compiled from official school websites.
Clues intentionally avoid revealing enrollment size, grade span, or directional/proximity information because those are gameplay feedback channels.

clues.text: Its mission says the school ensures a standard of academic excellence by providing a nurturing environment that allows each child to reach their fullest potential as students and citizens.
Official materials say the school has been operating since 1998 and is located in the heart of downtown Statesville.
The school highlights both the arts and STEM as signature parts of its program.
Its arts page says the school has an 88-seat black box theater used for student performances and community events.
A capital campaign page says the American Renaissance Arts and Science Center is located in the restored Statesville Record and Landmark building.
Official board pages list Paul Renegar as Board Chair.
Its public-facing motto is "Elevating Education, Building Character."
The school says it shapes instruction around each child's strengths and abilities and integrates Moral Focus lessons into every school day.
Its board documents state that the school, in cooperation with parents and community, strives to challenge students to reach their highest potential in academic excellence and moral character.
The leadership page says the school board is made up of local civic and community leaders who volunteer their time and energy.
Its homepage cites a Stanford University study saying National Heritage Academies partner-school students gained the equivalent of 3.5 months of additional learning each year.
Official pages list Tracey Duhaime as principal and Brian Clarida as board president.
Its mission says the school builds a rigorous and productive learning environment where students can reach their academic potential and teachers set clear educational goals.
Official materials say the school values the relationship between teacher and student and the role the family plays in academic and social development.
The school says citizenship and experiential learning through community engagement are woven throughout the student experience.
Governance pages say the school is governed by the state charter educational foundation and managed with professional support from Charter Schools USA.
Its homepage says the class of 2026 earned over 10 million dollars in scholarships.
Official staff pages list Jennifer Wilson as Lower Campus Principal and Nicole Sanfilippo as Upper Campus Principal.
Its homepage calls the school the Home of the Cougars.
Official materials say it was founded in 2006 by a group of Johnston County parents who wanted a school of choice and that it was the first charter school in the county.
The school says it is the only locally operated, non-profit public K-12 charter school in Johnston County.
Its board page organizes board priorities around educational excellence, growth planning, financial sustainability, leadership development, and operational excellence.
Official board materials say the board is made up of community leaders who meet monthly and provide oversight, support, and expertise.
Official pages list Michelle Denning as Board President and Tyler Grady as Upper Academy Director.
Its public-facing tagline is "We Place Children First."
A charter renewal announcement says the school opened in 2006.
Official news says the school received a 10-year charter renewal in 2026, the longest term available for charter schools.
The school has an RCS Sports Hall of Fame and publicizes induction ceremonies for former student athletes.
A leadership announcement says Darkarai Bryant was named Managing Executive Director after serving as the academy's upper-school principal.
Official staff pages list Darkarai Bryant as Managing Executive Director alongside division leaders Jamel Jones and Jeremy Martin.
